questionText: Let $P$ be 'the integer $n$ is a multiple of $9$' and let $Q$ be 'the digit sum of $n$ is divisible by $9$'. Determine whether $P$ is a sufficient condition for $Q$, justifying your answer. Comment also on whether $P$ is necessary for $Q$.

steps.workingLatex: P:\ 9 \mid n, \qquad Q:\ 9 \mid (\text{digit sum of } n)
steps.explanation: We first pin down each claim. P is about the number itself being divisible by $9$; Q is about the sum of its digits being divisible by $9$.

steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient for } Q \iff P \Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: Saying P is sufficient for Q means P on its own guarantees Q. So the task reduces to checking whether the forward implication holds.

A standard result says a number is divisible by $9$ exactly when its digit sum is. This is because every power of ten leaves remainder $1$ on division by $9$, so $n$ and its digit sum share the same remainder.

steps.description: Apply the rule to test P ⇒ Q, with an example.
steps.explanation: If $n$ is a multiple of $9$ then the rule immediately gives that its digit sum is divisible by $9$; the case $n=72$ illustrates it. So P forces Q.

steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \;\Rightarrow\; P \text{ is sufficient for } Q
steps.explanation: Because being a multiple of $9$ guarantees a digit sum divisible by $9$, P is indeed a sufficient condition for Q.

steps.description: Test the reverse direction Q ⇒ P.
steps.explanation: The divisibility rule is itself a biconditional, so a digit sum divisible by $9$ also forces $n$ to be a multiple of $9$. This means the reverse implication holds too.

steps.description: Interpret the reverse direction as necessity.
steps.workingLatex: Q \Rightarrow P \;\Rightarrow\; P \text{ is also necessary for } Q
steps.explanation: Since Q cannot hold without P, P is necessary as well. Here the condition is in fact both necessary and sufficient.
P is at least sufficient for Q, and because the divisibility rule works both ways it is necessary too, giving a full equivalence.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Yes, $P$ is sufficient for $Q$: any multiple of $9$ has a digit sum divisible by $9$ (the divisibility rule). In fact the rule works both ways, so $P$ is also necessary; thus $P \Leftrightarrow Q$.
